preamble: The voiced dental fricative, represented by the IPA symbol /ð/, can undergo a phonetic transformation to a voiced retroflex flap /ɽ/ in certain linguistic contexts. This shift is observed in specific dialects, where the dental fricative is replaced by a sound produced further back in the mouth with a different manner of articulation.
phoneticEffects: This transformation involves a change in the manner of articulation from a fricative to a flap, which is a form of lenition

examples.note: as in 'the', where the voiced dental fricative /ð/ is realized as a retroflex flap /ɽ/.
as in 'than', where the voiced dental fricative /ð/ is realized as a retroflex flap /ɽ/.
as in 'then', where the voiced dental fricative /ð/ is realized as a retroflex flap /ɽ/.

sources: Hrytsyna, I. (2017). The evolution of English dental fricatives: variation and change. ResearchGate.
Weldon, T. L. (2004). Gullah: Phonology. In Kortmann, B. & Schneider, E. W. (eds.), A handbook of varieties of English. A multimedia reference tool, Vol. 1, 393–406. Berlin: Mouton de Gruyter.
